{{< include latex-macros/macros.qmd >}}

:::{#exr-logistic-coef-interp}

For @eq-wcgs-model-1,
derive interpretations of $\beta_0$, $\beta_P$, $\beta_A$, and $\beta_{PA}$ 
on the odds **and** log-odds scales, 
State the interpretations concisely in math **and** in words.

*(Hint: apply the [general procedure for interpreting a regression coefficient](Linear-models-overview.qmd#def-coef-interp-procedure) to the log-odds linear predictor $\eta(\vx) = \logit(\pi(\vx))$.)*

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#sol-logistic-coef-interp}

\ 

```{r}
# include: false
age_offset = 0L
```

$$
\ba
\eta(P=0,A=0) &= \beta_0 + \beta_P \cdot 0 + \beta_A \cdot 0 + \beta_{PA} (0 \cdot 0)
&& \text{(model equation, with } p = 0, a = 0 \text{)}
\\
&= \beta_0 + 0 + 0 + 0
&& \text{(multiplication by zero)}
\\
&= \beta_0
&& \text{(additive identity)}
\ea
$$

Therefore:

$$\beta_{0} = \eta(P=0,A=0)$${#eq-wcgs-intxn-beta0}

$\beta_{0}$ is the natural logarithm of the odds ("log-odds") of experiencing CHD for a `r age_offset` year-old person with a type B personality; that is,

::: notes

The WCGS cohort ranges in age from 39 to 59 years,
so a `r age_offset`-year-old is far outside the observed data.
This intercept is therefore an extrapolation with no in-sample referent.
Centering age at a representative value
(replacing $A$ with $A - \bar{A}$)
is the standard remedy:
it makes the intercept the log-odds at the mean age,
which does have in-sample support.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

$\text{e}^{\beta_{0}}$ is the odds of experiencing CHD
for a `r age_offset` year-old with a type B personality.
Here we write

$$\odds(p, a) \eqdef \oddst(Y=1|P=p, A=a)$$

for the odds of CHD at covariate values $(p, a)$.
Then:

$$
\ba
\expf{\beta_{0}}
&= \expf{\eta(P=0,A=0)}
&& \text{(} \beta_0 \text{ as a log-odds)}
\\ &= \odds(P=0,A=0)
&& \text{(exponential of log-odds is odds: } \odds = \expf{\eta} \text{)}
\\ &= \frac{\Pr(Y= 1| P = 0, A = 0)}{1-\Pr(Y= 1| P = 0, A = 0)}
&& \text{(definition of odds)}
\\ &= \frac
{\Pr(Y= 1| P = 0, A = 0)}
{\Pr(Y= 0| P = 0, A = 0)}
&& \text{(complement rule: } 1 - \Pr(Y=1|\cdot) = \Pr(Y=0|\cdot) \text{)}
\ea
$$

::: notes

As with the log-odds interpretation,
this odds is evaluated at age `r age_offset`,
which lies outside the cohort's 39-to-59-year age range,
so $\text{e}^{\beta_0}$ has no in-sample referent.
Centering age before fitting
gives an intercept whose odds is evaluated at the mean age instead.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

$$
\ba
\deriv{a}\eta(P=0, A = a)
&= \deriv{a} \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_P \cdot 0 + \beta_A a + \beta_{PA}(0\cdot a)}
&& \text{(model equation, with } p = 0 \text{)}
\\
&= \deriv{a} \paren{\beta_0 + 0 + \beta_A a + 0}
&& \text{(multiplication by zero)}
\\
&= \deriv{a} \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_A a}
&& \text{(additive identity)}
\\
&= \deriv{a}\beta_0 + \deriv{a}\paren{\beta_A a}
&& \text{(linearity of differentiation)}
\\
&=  0 + \beta_A
&& \text{(constants have derivative 0; derivative of a linear term)}
\\
&= \beta_A
&& \text{(additive identity)}
\ea
$$

Therefore:

$$\beta_A = \deriv{a}\eta(P=0, A = a)$${#eq-wcgs-intxn-betaA}

$\beta_A$ is the slope of the log-odds of CHD with respect to age, for individuals with personality type B.

Alternatively:

$$
\ba
\eta(P = 0, A = a + 1)- \eta(P = 0, A = a)
&= \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_P \cdot 0 + \beta_A (a+1) + \beta_{PA} (0 \cdot (a+1))} - \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_P \cdot 0 + \beta_A a + \beta_{PA} (0 \cdot a)}
&& \text{(model equation, with } p = 0 \text{)}
\\
&= \paren{\beta_0 + 0 + \beta_A (a+1) + 0} - \paren{\beta_0 + 0 + \beta_A a + 0}
&& \text{(multiplication by zero)}
\\
&= \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_A (a+1)} - \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_A a}
&& \text{(additive identity)}
\\
&= \beta_0 + \beta_A a + \beta_A - \beta_0 - \beta_A a
&& \text{(distribute)}
\\
&= \beta_{A}
&& \text{(cancel } \beta_0 \text{ and } \beta_A a \text{)}
\ea
$$

That is,
$\beta_{A}$ is the difference in 
log-odds of experiencing CHD
per one-year difference in age 
between two individuals with type B personalities.

{{< slidebreak >}}

$$
\ba
\expf{\beta_{A}} &= \expf{\eta(P = 0, A = a + 1)- \eta(P = 0, A = a)}
&& \text{(} \beta_A \text{ as a difference in log-odds)}
\\
&= \frac{\expf{\eta(P = 0, A = a + 1)}}{\expf{\eta(P = 0, A = a)}}
&& \text{(exponential of a difference is a ratio)}
\\
&= \frac{\odds(P = 0, A = a + 1)}{\odds(P = 0, A = a)}
&& \text{(exponential of log-odds is odds: } \odds = \expf{\eta} \text{)}
\\
&= \frac
{\oddst(Y= 1|P=0, A = a + 1)}
{\oddst(Y= 1|P=0, A = a)}
&& \text{(the } \odds(p, a) \text{ notation defined at } \beta_0 \text{'s interpretation)}
\\
&= \ror(\Delta a = 1 | P = 0)
&& \text{(definition of the odds ratio)}
\ea
$$

* The odds ratio of experiencing CHD (aka "the odds ratio") 
differs by a factor of $\text{e}^{\beta_{A}}$ 
per one-year difference in age 
between individuals with type B personality.

{{< slidebreak >}}

$\beta_{P}$ is the difference in log-odds of experiencing CHD
for a `r age_offset` year-old person with type A personality
compared to a `r age_offset` year-old person with type B personality;
that is,

$$\b_P = \eta(P = 1, A = 0) - \eta(P=0, A = 0)$${#eq-wcgs-intxn-betaP}

{{< slidebreak >}}

-   $\text{e}^{\beta_{P}}$ is the ratio of the odds (aka "the odds ratio")
    of experiencing CHD, 
    for a `r age_offset`-year old individual with type A personality 
    vs a `r age_offset`-year old individual with type B personality; that is,

$$
\expf{\beta_{P}}
= \frac
{\oddst(Y= 1|P=1, A = 0)}
{\oddst(Y= 1|P=0, A = 0)}
$$

{{< slidebreak >}}

$$
\ba
\deriv{a}\eta(P=\red{1}, A = a)
&= \deriv{a} \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_P \cdot 1 + \beta_A a + \beta_{PA}(1 \cdot a)}
&& \text{(model equation, with } p = 1 \text{)}
\\
&= \deriv{a} \paren{\beta_0 + \beta_P + \beta_A a + \beta_{PA} a}
&& \text{(multiplicative identity)}
\\
&= \deriv{a}\beta_0 + \deriv{a}\beta_P + \deriv{a}\paren{\beta_A a} + \deriv{a}\paren{\beta_{PA} a}
&& \text{(linearity of differentiation)}
\\
&= 0 + 0 + \beta_A + \beta_{PA}
&& \text{(constants have derivative 0; derivatives of linear terms)}
\\
&= \red{\beta_A + \beta_{PA}}
&& \text{(additive identity)}
\\
\deriv{a}\eta(P=\teal{0}, A = a) &= \teal{\beta_A}
&& \text{(the type B slope, from the } \beta_A \text{ derivation)}
\ea
$$

Therefore:

$$
\ba
\deriv{a}\eta(P=\red{1}, A = a) - \deriv{a}\eta(P=\teal{0}, A = a)
&= \paren{\red{\beta_A + \beta_{PA}}} - \teal{\beta_A}
&& \text{(substitute the two slopes)}
\\
&= \beta_A - \beta_A + \beta_{PA}
&& \text{(commute the addition)}
\\
&= \beta_{PA}
&& \text{(cancel } \beta_A \text{)}
\ea
$$

That is,

$$
\ba
\beta_{PA} &= \deriv{a}\eta(P=\red{1}, A = a) - \deriv{a}\eta(P=\teal{0}, A = a)
&& \text{(rearrange the difference of slopes)}
\\
&= \deriv{a}\eta(P=\red{1}, A = a) - \teal{\beta_A}
&& \text{(substitute the type B slope, } \deriv{a}\eta(P=0, A=a) = \beta_A \text{)}
\ea
$$

$\beta_{PA}$ is the difference in
the slopes of log-odds over age between
participants with Type A personalities and
participants with Type B personalities.

{{< slidebreak >}}

Accordingly, 
the odds ratio of experiencing CHD per one-year difference in age
differs by a factor of $\text{e}^{\beta_{PA}}$
for participants with type A personality compared to
participants with type B personality; that is,

$$
\ba
\ror(\Delta a = 1 | P = 1)
= \expf{\beta_{PA}} \times \ror(\Delta a = 1 | P = 0)
\ea
$$

or equivalently:

$$
\expf{\beta_{PA}} = 
\frac
{\ror(\Delta a = 1 | P = 1)}
{\ror(\Delta a = 1 | P = 0)}
$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

See [Section 5.1.1](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4614-1353-0_5#Sec2_5)
of @vittinghoff2e for another perspective,
also using the `wcgs` data as an example.
